retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- Small Interfering RNA
retrieved_docs.content: Small Interfering RNAs (siRNA) are double-stranded, non-coding RNAs that inhibit gene expression through RNA interference. They interfere with gene expression by degrading mRNA and preventing the translation of proteins. siRNAs form from long double-stranded RNAs with the assistance of Dicer. Once fully formed, siRNA binds to an RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) and cleaves mRNA through a catalytic RISC protein, Argonaute. [15] Small interfering RNAs have the potential to be therapeutic agents for diseases due to their potency and ability to knock down genes. Unlike miRNAs, siRNAs can specifically target a gene of choice, and a single siRNA guide strand can function multiple times. [16]

retrieved_docs.content: Adverse events are listed by the agent below. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Pathophysiology -- Loss of Expression
retrieved_docs.content: Deletion of a cellular receptor gene results in the complete loss of expression. Epigenetic modifications, such as methylation and histone modification, can also silence a receptor gene, leading to reduced expression. This loss of expression leads to loss of function and downregulation of signaling pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: On a molecular level, pseudogenes have associations with several roles. Numerous studies have revealed that specific genes and their associated pseudogenes display regulatory roles in the cell. Some pseudogenes have exhibited antisense RNA properties, siRNA properties, and even an ability to affect mRNA stability. Through these molecular functions, pseudogene transcripts can modulate the number of parental copy transcripts expressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The short double-strand RNA fragments integrate with a cluster of essential proteins known as the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) and are then split into 2 single RNA strands recognized as sense and antisense. The single antisense strand further targets its mRNA arrangement while the sense strand is dismissed from the complex. The essential enzymes in the RISC complex, such as the Ago-2 endonucleases, cleave their target mRNA sequence once affixed. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Nedosiran
retrieved_docs.content: Injection site reactions (≥20%) [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: siRNAs additionally regulate gene expression. In one study, pseudogene transcripts were observed to form hairpin structures through folding and become functional siRNAs that repressed gene expression. The study additionally removed Dicer, a protein responsible for producing siRNAs, and observed a decreased concentration of pseudogene-derived siRNAs with subsequent increased expression of the coding gene’s mRNA products. The study further supports the idea of pseudogene derived siRNA regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- Small Nuclear RNA
retrieved_docs.content: Small nuclear RNAs (snRNA) are non-coding RNAs that are responsible for splicing introns. The snRNAs join with proteins to form small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNP), which most commonly contain U1, U2, U4, U5, and U6 snRNA molecules. [12] Spliceosome assembly and activity begin once U1 of the snRNP binds a complementary sequence on the 5’ splice site of a pre-mRNA transcript. Introns are then removed from the pre-mRNA transcript by the spliceosome complex, and mature mRNA forms.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the intricate pharmacology of siRNA is crucial for healthcare professionals to deliver precise therapeutic interventions in various clinical scenarios. This activity equips participants with comprehensive knowledge encompassing the mechanism of action, potential adverse reactions, and contraindications, empowering them to integrate advanced therapeutic strategies into patient care. This comprehension ensures that professionals possess the requisite expertise to provide optimal and innovative patient care within the dynamic realm of medical science, thereby enhancing patient outcomes and contributing to the advancement of medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Administration -- Vutrisiran
retrieved_docs.content: It is recommended that the recommended amount of vitamin A be supplemented daily while taking vutrisiran. If any ocular symptoms that suggest a vitamin A deficiency occur, it is recommended to consult an ophthalmologist. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, pseudogenes were referred to as "junk DNA" due to their location in non-coding sequences of the genome. However, recent studies have begun to unravel various functions of pseudogenes and their mRNA transcripts. These functions vary in regulatory roles, and they include antisense RNA, siRNA, miRNA-like, and miRNA binding or inhibiting properties.

retrieved_docs.content: Injection site reactions (38%) Abdominal pain/discomfort (15%)

retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the mechanism of action of small interfering RNA (siRNA)-based therapies. Identify the appropriate monitoring for patients receiving small interfering RNA (siRNA)-based therapies. Assess the potential adverse effects of small interfering RNA (siRNA)-based therapies. Implement interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance small interfering RNA (siRNA)-based therapies in treating patients with various conditions. retrieved_docs.title: Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) Therapy -- Administration -- Inclisiran
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran is available and distributed as a pre-filled single-dose vial of 284 mg/1.5 mL (189 mg/mL) for subcutaneous injection. Indication: reducing LDL-C in subjects with HeFH or ASCVD adjunctive to diet and statin therapy tolerated at a maximum dose. 284 mg (initial dose) 284 mg (at 3 months) 284 mg (every 6 months after that)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: sex‑hormone framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 518
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified as a member of the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified within the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system, indicating that it acts on hormonal pathways related to reproductive function. In addition, mifepristone functions as a hormone antagonist, meaning it blocks the action of certain endogenous hormones, particularly progesterone, thereby influencing processes that depend on progesterone signaling. This dual characterization highlights mifepristone’s role both as a member of a specific pharmacologic class and as an antagonist of hormonal activity, which is central to its therapeutic applications in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as members of the progesterone family, indicating that they share structural and functional characteristics with the hormone progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist, indicating that it functions by blocking hormone receptors. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action and is also categorized as a hormone antagonist. These relationships show that both PD 134308 and atosiban share the functional property of antagonizing hormone receptors, a characteristic that is important for therapeutic approaches aimed at modulating hormone‑mediated physiological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Androgens and estrogens are classified as members of the broader hormone group that includes estrogens and testosterone, reflecting their role as sex steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1520
retrieved_docs.content: Inosine deficiency, deficiency of sex steroid hormones, deficiency of sulfur, and deficiency of testosterone are all recognized as allowed qualifiers. These qualifiers denote specific deficits—inosine deficiency refers to a lack of the nucleoside inosine, while deficiency of sex steroid hormones and testosterone indicates insufficient levels of hormones that regulate sexual development and function. Deficiency of sulfur highlights a shortfall of this essential element, which is important for numerous biochemical processes. Recognizing these deficiencies as allowed qualifiers helps clinicians identify and categorize metabolic and hormonal insufficiencies that may require targeted evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 434
retrieved_docs.content: Estren derivatives, sex hormones and modulators of the genital system are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are likewise classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are identified as members of the progesterone group. Together, these relationships indicate that both Estren derivatives, sex hormones and modulators of the genital system and Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens belong to the broader category of progestogen hormones that regulate genital function, with Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens specifically falling within the progesterone subclass.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic estrogens, plain are classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. As a synthetic form of these hormones, they function within the same physiological pathways as natural estrogens, influencing reproductive tissue development and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Medroxyprogesterone is classified as a member of the progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives group, indicating that it belongs to the class of systemic hormonal contraceptives that contain progestogen agents. This relationship places medroxyprogesterone within the broader category of progestogen-based contraceptive methods used for systemic regulation of fertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Androgeny and the class of compounds known as androgens are both classified as forms of nandrolone, a synthetic anabolic‑androgenic steroid. As variants of nandrolone, they share the characteristic hormonal activity of this compound, which includes promoting protein synthesis, increasing muscle mass, and exerting androgenic effects. Recognizing androgeny and androgens as types of nandrolone helps clarify their pharmacologic profile and informs clinical decisions regarding their therapeutic use, potential side‑effects, and regulatory considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: The substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, known as atosiban, functions as an inverse ISA, indicating that it antagonizes peptide hormone receptors while exhibiting inverse agonist properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2746
retrieved_docs.content: Progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives constitute a class of systemic birth‑control agents that rely on synthetic progestogen compounds, and norethindrone is a specific progestogen that belongs to this class, illustrating the pharmacologic composition of these contraceptive methods.
